skill.name: datadog-automation
skill.description: Automate Datadog monitoring tasks through Rube MCP, including metrics, logs, monitors, dashboards, events, downtimes, hosts, and traces.
skill.summary: Guide Datadog operations through Rube MCP with current tool schemas and workflow safeguards.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static external command, weak crypto, and reconnaissance findings are false positives caused by Markdown backticks, Datadog query examples, parameter names, and tool names. The real concern is operational: the skill routes work through an external Rube MCP endpoint and can guide monitor, dashboard, event, and downtime changes in Datadog.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Service Handles Datadog Operations
medium_findings.description: The setup instructs users to add https://rube.app/mcp as an MCP server. This is expected for the skill, but Datadog requests and results may pass through an external service, so users should understand the data path and authorization model.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded external MCP URL is explicit and semantically required for the skill. The risk depends on user authorization and service trust, not hidden behavior.

medium_findings.title: Datadog Write Actions Can Change Monitoring State
medium_findings.description: The skill documents workflows for creating and updating monitors, muting monitors, updating or deleting dashboards, creating events, and scheduling downtimes. These are legitimate Datadog automation capabilities, but mistakes can suppress alerts, remove dashboards, or change incident signals.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed tool sequences include multiple write or destructive Datadog actions. The skill also warns that dashboard deletion is irreversible, confirming operational impact.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby backtick findings are Markdown inline code or fenced examples for MCP tool names, Datadog queries, parameter names, and tag syntax. I did not find evidence of actual Ruby code or shell command execution in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is Markdown documentation, not executable Ruby. There are no scripts, code files, or command invocation constructs present.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Matches Are Token Collisions
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits align with ordinary words or parameter values such as description, asc, and desc. The reconnaissance hits align with Datadog host and monitor tool names, not local system enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain Datadog documentation terms and tool names only. I did not find cryptographic implementation, credential harvesting, or host reconnaissance code.

dangerous_patterns.title: External MCP Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requires an external Rube MCP endpoint for Datadog automation. This pattern should be disclosed because observability data and actions may flow through the MCP service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint is directly documented and required. It is not malicious by itself, but it is a real network trust dependency.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Datadog Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflows include deleting dashboards and creating downtimes. These actions can remove visibility or suppress alerts when used with broad scope or wrong identifiers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly lists deletion and downtime workflows and warns that deletion is permanent. The operational risk is clear even though the intent is legitimate.

content.user_title: Automate Datadog Operations with Rube MCP
content.value_statement: Datadog operations often require exact tool schemas, timestamps, and query syntax. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for safer Datadog automation through Rube MCP.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup of the Rube MCP server and Datadog connection checks.
Requires searching current Rube tool schemas before running Datadog workflows.
Documents metric query, log search, monitor, dashboard, event, downtime, host, and trace workflows.
Lists key parameters for common Datadog operations, including timestamps, queries, tags, and IDs.
Highlights operational pitfalls such as timestamp units, monitor type mismatches, retention limits, and irreversible dashboard deletion.
limitations: Requires an active Rube MCP connection and an authorized Datadog account.
Does not include executable automation code or local validation scripts.
Tool schemas may change, so the assistant must search Rube tools before acting.
Write actions depend on Datadog permissions and can affect production monitoring.

use_cases.title: Investigate Incident Signals
use_cases.description: Query metrics, search logs, inspect traces, and list hosts while preserving Datadog query and time range conventions.

use_cases.title: Maintain Monitoring Coverage
use_cases.description: Review monitors, update thresholds, mute alerts temporarily, and document changes before production rollout.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Maintenance Windows
use_cases.description: Create events and downtimes with scoped tags, clear start and end times, and documented service impact.

prompt_templates.title: Check Datadog Connection
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Datadog automation skill to verify Rube MCP is available, search for current Datadog tools, and confirm my Datadog connection status before taking any action.

prompt_templates.title: Search Recent Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Datadog logs for errors from service <service-name> during the last <time-window>. Show the query, index assumptions, result summary, and any pagination limits.

prompt_templates.title: Review Monitor Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Find monitors tagged <tag>. Summarize their alert queries, thresholds, notification targets, and any risks before suggesting updates.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Maintenance Downtime
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a Datadog downtime for scope <scope> from <start-time> to <end-time>. Search current tool schemas first, show the exact scope, and wait for confirmation before creating it.

output_examples.input: Find recent API errors in Datadog.
output: Confirms the current Datadog log search tool schema through Rube MCP.
Builds a Datadog log query for the requested service and time window.
Summarizes error counts, common messages, affected hosts, and any retention or pagination limits.

output_examples.input: Create a downtime for staging hosts tonight.
output: Identifies the required downtime fields, including scope, start time, end time, and message.
Warns if the end time is missing or the scope is too broad.
Requests confirmation before creating the downtime in Datadog.

output_examples.input: Review dashboards for the payments service.
output: Lists matching dashboards and highlights IDs, titles, and relevant widgets.
Explains any update or deletion risk before proposing changes.
Keeps dashboard modifications separate from read-only review steps.

best_practices: Search current Rube tool schemas before every Datadog workflow.
Confirm scope, timestamps, IDs, and notification targets before any write action.
Use narrow time windows and tag filters to reduce noisy or expensive queries.
anti_patterns: Creating indefinite downtimes without an explicit end time.
Deleting dashboards without exporting or reviewing the existing dashboard first.
Running broad log or host searches without indexes, tags, or time limits.

faq.question: Does this skill connect directly to Datadog?
faq.answer: No. It guides Datadog operations through Rube MCP and requires an active Datadog connection in that service.

faq.question: Can it modify production monitors?
faq.answer: Yes, if the connected Datadog account has permission. Confirm every monitor write action before execution.

faq.question: Why must tools be searched first?
faq.answer: Rube MCP tool schemas can change. Searching first reduces errors from stale parameters or outdated workflows.

faq.question: Does it manage Datadog credentials?
faq.answer: No. Authentication is handled through Rube MCP and the Datadog connection flow.

faq.question: What are the main safety concerns?
faq.answer: Broad downtimes, monitor mutations, dashboard deletion, and external MCP data flow need review before use.

faq.question: Can it help with read-only investigations?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports metric queries, log searches, dashboard review, host listing, and trace lookup workflows.
